subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Think about the moment the sun sets over Hargeisa or Mogadishu and the sky turns dark. What happens next in your home? Someone switches on a light, or you press the button on a torch, or your phone screen glows so you can keep reading. All of these use electricity. Electricity is one of the most useful and important discoveries in human history, and in this unit you will find out what it really is, how it flows, and how we use it safely every single day.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain that electric current is the flow of tiny particles called electrons around a complete circuit.
Describe the job of a cell and a battery, and identify the positive (+) and negative (-) terminals.
Explain that opposite charges attract and like charges repel, and use this to say which way electrons move.
Recognise and draw the standard circuit symbols for a cell, battery, switch, lamp, buzzer and ammeter.
Draw and read a circuit diagram, and build a real circuit from a diagram.
State that current is measured in amps (A) using an ammeter connected in series.
State the rule that current is the same at every point in a series circuit.
Predict what happens to current when you add or remove cells and lamps.
Distinguish between electrical conductors and insulators and explain the difference in terms of electrons.

concepts.title: What Is Electric Current?
concepts.explanation: Let us start with the big idea. When you close the switch on a torch, the bulb lights up almost instantly. But what actually travels through the wires to make that happen? The answer is electrons. Everything around you — the wire, the wall, your own hand, the air, a cup of camel milk — is made of atoms. Atoms are far too small to see. Inside every atom there are even smaller particles, and one type is called the electron. Electrons carry a negative charge. In most materials the electrons are held tightly in place,…
concepts.example: When there is no cell connected, these free electrons in a metal wire move about randomly, like a crowd of goats milling around a market with no particular direction. Nothing useful happens. But the moment you connect a…

concepts.title: Circuit Symbols and Circuit Diagrams
concepts.explanation: If you wanted to explain a circuit to your friend Farah in Berbera, you could draw a picture of the real bulb, the real switch and the real cell. But real components come in many shapes — a lamp in a torch looks very different from a ceiling light, yet they do the same job. Drawing detailed pictures is slow and can be confusing. So scientists agreed long ago to use simple standard circuit symbols instead. The wonderful thing is that these symbols are the same in every country in the world. An engineer in Nairobi,…
concepts.example: Here are the most important symbols you must learn. Because we describe them in words, picture each one carefully and practise drawing it:

concepts.title: Measuring Current with an Ammeter
concepts.explanation: We have said that current is the flow of electrons. But how much current is flowing? When the electrons move more quickly, the current is larger. When they move more slowly, the current is smaller. To put a number on it, we measure current in units called amps, which has the symbol A. (Amps is the short, common form of the full word amperes.) A small torch bulb might use a fraction of an amp; a phone charger uses about 1.5 A. We measure current with an ammeter. A real ammeter has two terminals for the wires,…
concepts.example: An ammeter must always be connected in series with the other components. "In series" means the components are joined end-to-end, one after another, in a single loop with no branches. The current has to flow through the…

concepts.title: Conductors and Insulators
concepts.explanation: Not every material lets electricity flow. Try to imagine testing different things around your home by putting them into a simple circuit with a bulb. A metal spoon makes the bulb light. A wooden spoon does not. Why? A conductor is a material that allows current to flow. This happens because a conductor has electrons that are free to move. When you connect a conductor into a circuit, its free electrons join the flow and the current passes through. All metals are conductors — copper, aluminium, steel, silver and…
concepts.example: An insulator is a material that does not allow current to flow. In an insulator the electrons are not free to move — they are locked in place — so there is nothing to carry the current. Most non-metals are insulators:…

concepts.title: Changing a Circuit - Adding Cells and Lamps
concepts.explanation: What happens to the current if you change a circuit? The answer depends on what you change. Let us look at the two most common changes. A cell is a store of chemical energy. If you add more cells to a circuit (keeping everything else the same), you have more energy to push the electrons. So the electrons move faster and the current increases. With two cells instead of one, the current roughly doubles, and the lamp glows brighter. If instead you remove a cell, there is less energy, the current decreases, and the…
concepts.example: But be careful! Adding too many cells pushes too much current, which can make the wires get hot and can damage the lamp — the bulb may burn out with a bright flash. This is a small taste of why using the correct voltage…

concepts.title: Series and Parallel Circuits
concepts.explanation: So far we have mostly talked about series circuits, where everything is joined in one single loop. Series circuits have one big weakness: because there is only one path, if one component breaks or one lamp burns out, the loop is broken and everything stops. This is the reason that old strings of decorative lights would all go dark when just one tiny bulb failed — and finding the broken one was a frustrating job! There is another way to connect components: a parallel circuit. In a parallel circuit, components are…
concepts.example: This is why the electric lights in your home are wired in parallel, not in series. You want to be able to switch off the light in one room while the light in another room stays on. If one bulb fails, you do not want the…

explorations.title: Make a Simple Circuit Light a Bulb
explorations.context: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to show that the bulb only lights when the circuit is complete.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: What do you think must be true for the bulb to light? Complete this sentence — 'The bulb will only light if...'
explorations.answer: In which case did the bulb light? What does this tell you about a circuit? When you made a gap, why did the bulb go out immediately? Which parts of your circuit were the conductors that carried the current?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to show that the bulb only lights when the circuit is complete.

explorations.title: Which Materials Are Conductors?
explorations.context: To test everyday materials and sort them into conductors and insulators.
explorations.prompt: Predict which of your test objects will let the bulb light. Write down your predictions before testing, and give a reason for each.
explorations.answer: What do all the conductors have in common? Explain, using electrons, why the conductors let the bulb light. Why is it important to touch the wires together first, before testing objects? The pencil graphite may surprise you. Was it a conductor or an insulator? (Graphite is a non-metal that…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To test everyday materials and sort them into conductors and insulators.

explorations.title: Static Electricity with a Balloon or Comb
explorations.context: To create static electricity and observe how charged objects attract light things.
explorations.prompt: Predict: 'When I rub the balloon on my hair and hold it near small bits of paper, I think...' Write your prediction before you start.
explorations.answer: What happened to the paper bits? Why were they attracted to the balloon? Rubbing moves electrons onto the balloon. What charge does the balloon gain? Why did the balloon stick to the wall? Why does this experiment work much better in the dry season than in the humid, wet season near the coast?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To create static electricity and observe how charged objects attract light things.

explorations.title: Make a Simple Switch
explorations.context: To build a working switch and show how it turns a circuit on and off by completing or breaking the loop.
explorations.prompt: Predict how your switch will control the bulb. 'When the paperclip touches both drawing pins, the bulb will... When I lift it, the bulb will...'
explorations.answer: Why does the bulb light only when the paperclip touches both pins? In terms of the circuit loop, what is the switch actually doing? Draw the circuit diagram for your switch circuit using the correct symbols.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a working switch and show how it turns a circuit on and off by completing or breaking the loop.

methods.title: Make a Simple Circuit Light a Bulb
methods.example: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to show that the bulb only lights when the circuit is complete.
steps: Tape one wire firmly to the + (positive) end of the battery.
Tape the other end of that wire to the metal base or side of the bulb.
Tape a second wire to the other part of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the - (negative) end of the battery.
Observe: does the bulb light? Now lift the wire away from the battery to make a gap.

methods.title: Which Materials Are Conductors?
methods.example: To test everyday materials and sort them into conductors and insulators.
steps: First, touch the two free wire ends together to check the bulb lights. This proves your circuit works before you begin.
Now hold one test object so it bridges the gap, touching both wire ends.
Watch the bulb. If it lights, the object is a conductor. If it stays dark, it is an insulator.
Record your result, then test the next object. Test each object the same way (a fair test).
Observation / Recording Sheet

methods.title: Static Electricity with a Balloon or Comb
methods.example: To create static electricity and observe how charged objects attract light things.
steps: Rub the balloon (or comb) briskly on your dry hair for about ten seconds.
Hold the balloon just above the small pieces of paper and watch.
Now press the rubbed balloon against a wall and let go. Observe.
Rub again, then hold the balloon or comb near a thin stream of water from a tap (do this away from any electrical items). Watch the water.
Observation / Recording Sheet

methods.title: Make a Simple Switch
methods.example: To build a working switch and show how it turns a circuit on and off by completing or breaking the loop.
steps: Push the two drawing pins into the card a short distance apart. Hook one end of the paperclip under one pin so it can swing to touch the other pin.
Connect this switch into your circuit: one wire from the battery to the first pin, and a wire from the second pin onwards to the bulb and back to the battery.
Swing the paperclip so it touches both pins. Observe the bulb.
Swing the paperclip away so it touches only one pin. Observe again.
Observation / Recording Sheet

workedExamples.title: Reading and Reasoning About Current
workedExamples.solution: Question: An ammeter has a scale from 0 to 10. There are numbers at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10, and one small mark exactly halfway between each pair of numbers. The pointer sits on the small mark between 6 and 8. What is the current? Step 1 — Find the value of each small division. Between 6 and 8 there is a gap of 2 amps, and one small mark divides it into two equal parts. So each small part is worth 2 divided by 2 = 1 A. Step 2 — Read the pointer. The pointer is one small mark above 6, which means 6 + 1 = 7 A. Answer:…

workedExamples.title: 1 - Reading an ammeter
workedExamples.prompt: Question: An ammeter has a scale from 0 to 10. There are numbers at 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10, and one small mark exactly halfway between each pair of numbers. The pointer sits on the small mark between 6 and 8. What is the current?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 — Find the value of each small division. Between 6 and 8 there is a gap of 2 amps, and one small mark divides it into two equal parts. So each small part is worth 2 divided by 2 = 1 A. Step 2 — Read the pointer. The pointer is one small mark above 6, which means 6 + 1 = 7 A. Answer: the current is 7 A. Always remember to write the unit A. Worked Example 2 - Current in a series circuit

workedExamples.title: 2 - Current in a series circuit
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Halima builds a series circuit with a cell, a switch and two lamps. She places an ammeter just after the cell and reads 0.6 A. She then moves the ammeter to a position between the two lamps. What will it read there?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 — Recall the rule. In a series circuit the current is the same at every point. Step 2 — Apply it. Since the current is the same everywhere in the loop, moving the ammeter to a different position does not change the reading. Answer: the ammeter still reads 0.6 A. The lamps use energy, but they do not reduce the current. Worked Example 3 - Finding current from power (a real-life calculation)

workedExamples.title: 3 - Finding current from power (a real-life calculation)
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Idris reads that an electric fan is labelled 230 W, and the mains voltage in his home is 230 V. Roughly how much current does the fan use? (This is the calculation used when families think about their electricity bill.)
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 — Use the rule. You can estimate the current by dividing the watts by the volts: current = watts divided by volts. Step 2 — Put in the numbers. Current = 230 divided by 230 = 1 A. Answer: the fan uses about 1 A. A device labelled 460 W on the same 230 V supply would use about 2 A — double the current, because it uses energy twice as fast. Note: this calculation is only for understanding your bill. You must never open or test mains appliances yourself. Part 4: Conductors and Insulators

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Current is measured in: (a) volts (b) joules (c) amps (d) newtons
workedExamples.solution: 5 (c) amps Current is measured in amps, symbol A.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: An ammeter must be connected: (a) in series (b) on its own branch (c) across the cell only (d) with wet hands
workedExamples.solution: 6 (a) in series An ammeter goes in series so all the current flows through it to be counted.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: In a series circuit, the current is: (a) largest near the cell (b) the same everywhere (c) used up by the lamp (d) zero after the lamp
workedExamples.solution: 7 (b) the same everywhere The golden rule of series circuits: current is equal at every point.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which is a conductor? (a) plastic (b) dry wood (c) copper (d) rubber
workedExamples.solution: 8 (c) copper Copper is a metal, so its electrons are free to move.

practice.prompt: Electric current is the flow of: (a) water (b) electrons (c) heat (d) light
practice.answer: 1 (b) electrons Current is the flow of electrons around the circuit.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these must be present for current to flow in a circuit? (a) a complete loop (b) a break in the wire (c) an open switch (d) an insulator gap
practice.answer: 2 (a) a complete loop Electrons can only flow in an unbroken, complete circuit.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The negative terminal of a cell: (a) pulls electrons in (b) pushes electrons out (c) makes new electrons (d) stores light
practice.answer: 3 (b) pushes electrons out Electrons are negative, so the negative terminal repels (pushes) them out.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Opposite charges: (a) repel (b) do nothing (c) attract (d) disappear
practice.answer: 4 (c) attract Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Current is measured in: (a) volts (b) joules (c) amps (d) newtons
practice.answer: 5 (c) amps Current is measured in amps, symbol A.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An ammeter must be connected: (a) in series (b) on its own branch (c) across the cell only (d) with wet hands
practice.answer: 6 (a) in series An ammeter goes in series so all the current flows through it to be counted.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a series circuit, the current is: (a) largest near the cell (b) the same everywhere (c) used up by the lamp (d) zero after the lamp
practice.answer: 7 (b) the same everywhere The golden rule of series circuits: current is equal at every point.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is a conductor? (a) plastic (b) dry wood (c) copper (d) rubber
practice.answer: 8 (c) copper Copper is a metal, so its electrons are free to move.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is an insulator? (a) steel (b) aluminium (c) plastic (d) silver
practice.answer: 9 (c) plastic Plastic is a non-metal; its electrons are not free to move.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Adding more cells to a circuit (other things unchanged) makes the current: (a) increase (b) decrease (c) stay the same (d) stop
practice.answer: 10 (a) increase More cells give more energy, pushing electrons faster.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Adding more lamps in series (other things unchanged) makes the current: (a) increase (b) decrease (c) stay the same (d) reverse
practice.answer: 11 (b) decrease More lamps in series make electrons flow more slowly.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: If one lamp breaks in a series circuit, the other lamps: (a) get brighter (b) stay the same (c) all go out (d) are unaffected
practice.answer: 12 (c) all go out A series circuit has one path; one break stops the whole loop.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Make a Simple Circuit Light a Bulb
activities.materials: One or two 1.5 V batteries (AA cells); A small torch bulb (or an LED from a broken torch); Two or three short pieces of wire (or thin strips cut from foil, or wire from an old cable stripped by an adult); Sticky tape; Optional: a paperclip to make a switch
steps: Tape one wire firmly to the + (positive) end of the battery.
Tape the other end of that wire to the metal base or side of the bulb.
Tape a second wire to the other part of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the - (negative) end of the battery.
Observe: does the bulb light? Now lift the wire away from the battery to make a gap.

activities.title: Which Materials Are Conductors?
activities.materials: Your working circuit from Experiment 1, but leave a gap where you will insert test objects; A collection of objects to test: metal spoon, metal key, aluminium foil, coin, plastic ruler, wooden stick, rubber band, dry cloth, paper, a nail, a pencil 'lead' (graphite)
steps: First, touch the two free wire ends together to check the bulb lights. This proves your circuit works before you begin.
Now hold one test object so it bridges the gap, touching both wire ends.
Watch the bulb. If it lights, the object is a conductor. If it stays dark, it is an insulator.
Record your result, then test the next object. Test each object the same way (a fair test).
Observation / Recording Sheet

activities.title: Static Electricity with a Balloon or Comb
activities.materials: An inflated balloon, or a plastic comb, or a plastic pen; Small torn pieces of paper (tiny scraps); Clean, dry hair (this works best in the dry season); A wall; Optional: a thin stream of water from a slowly running tap
steps: Rub the balloon (or comb) briskly on your dry hair for about ten seconds.
Hold the balloon just above the small pieces of paper and watch.
Now press the rubbed balloon against a wall and let go. Observe.
Rub again, then hold the balloon or comb near a thin stream of water from a tap (do this away from any electrical items). Watch the water.
Observation / Recording Sheet

activities.title: Make a Simple Switch
activities.materials: Your battery-and-bulb circuit from Experiment 1; A metal paperclip; Two metal drawing pins (or two small nails) and a small piece of card or wood; Tape
steps: Push the two drawing pins into the card a short distance apart. Hook one end of the paperclip under one pin so it can swing to touch the other pin.
Connect this switch into your circuit: one wire from the battery to the first pin, and a wire from the second pin onwards to the bulb and back to the battery.
Swing the paperclip so it touches both pins. Observe the bulb.
Swing the paperclip away so it touches only one pin. Observe again.
Observation / Recording Sheet

activities.title: Observe: Changing a Circuit - Adding Cells and Lamps
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of changing a circuit - adding cells and lamps around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Series and Parallel Circuits
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of series and parallel circuits around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: - Electrical Safety Rules 1. For all your experiments, use only small 1.5 V batteries and bulbs. NEVER use the mains sockets or the wires in the wall. 2. Never push anything into a wall socket except a proper plug. Never poke metal objects, wires, nails or pins into a socket. 3. Never touch switches, sockets, plugs or appliances with wet hands, and never use electrical items near water. Water conducts electricity. 4. Never use a device with a damaged or frayed cable, or a cracked plug. Tell an adult so it can be repaired or replaced. 5. Do not overload a socket with too many plugs. 6. If someone gets an electric shock, do NOT touch them. Switch off the power first, then call an adult for help. 7. Stay away from fallen power lines and electricity pylons. Assume every power line is live and deadly.

terms: Long thin line (+) next to a short thick line (-), with a gap.
Two or more cell symbols in a row.
A line with a break and a small raised lever.
The same, but the lever is down completing the line.
A circle with a cross inside.
A circle with a capital A inside.
A material whose electrons are not free to move, so no current flows (plastic, wood, air).
A device that measures current in amps; connected in series.
Components in one single loop with one path.

commonMistakes: 'Current is used up by the lamp.'
Electrons are already in the wire.
'Short line of the cell is positive.'
It is drawn the wrong way.

connections.text: Energy (Unit 3): cells store chemical energy that becomes electrical, light and heat energy.
conductors are metals because of their free electrons; this links to the properties of metals.
current depends on electrons, which are particles inside atoms.
solar panels, batteries, torches and phone chargers all use these ideas.
how electricity and solar power are spreading across East Africa.
Find out how a solar panel turns sunlight into electricity.

fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (a) increase More cells give more energy, pushing electrons faster.

fluency.prompt: Adding more lamps in series (other things unchanged) makes the current: (a) increase (b) decrease (c) stay the same (d) reverse
fluency.answer: 11 (b) decrease More lamps in series make electrons flow more slowly.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (b) decrease More lamps in series make electrons flow more slowly.

fluency.prompt: If one lamp breaks in a series circuit, the other lamps: (a) get brighter (b) stay the same (c) all go out (d) are unaffected
fluency.answer: 12 (c) all go out A series circuit has one path; one break stops the whole loop.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (c) all go out A series circuit has one path; one break stops the whole loop.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan's family uses a solar panel to charge a battery that lights their home. Explain the energy changes from sunlight to the light in the room.
realProblems.answer: Light energy from the sun is changed by the solar panel into electrical energy, which is stored as chemical energy in the battery, and later changed back into electrical energy and then into light (and some heat) in the lamp.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Light energy from the sun is changed by the solar panel into electrical energy, which is stored as chemical energy in the battery, and later changed back into electrical energy and then into light (and some heat) in the lamp.

realProblems.prompt: The lights in a home in Nairobi are wired in parallel. Explain why this is better than series wiring for a house.
realProblems.answer: In parallel, each light has its own branch, so one can be switched off or fail without turning off the others. In series, one failure would darken the whole house.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: In parallel, each light has its own branch, so one can be switched off or fail without turning off the others. In series, one failure would darken the whole house.

realProblems.prompt: A phone charger is labelled 230 W and the mains is 230 V. Estimate the current it uses. (Show your working.)
realProblems.answer: Current = watts divided by volts = 230 divided by 230 = 1 A. (Working shown.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Current = watts divided by volts = 230 divided by 230 = 1 A. (Working shown.)

realProblems.prompt: Amina rubs a balloon on her hair in the dry season and it sticks to the wall. Explain why, using the idea of static charge.
realProblems.answer: Rubbing moves electrons, giving the balloon a static charge. The charged balloon attracts the opposite charges in the wall (opposite charges attract), so it sticks.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Rubbing moves electrons, giving the balloon a static charge. The charged balloon attracts the opposite charges in the wall (opposite charges attract), so it sticks.

realProblems.prompt: Why do the metal power lines on tall pylons hang from ceramic or glass insulators?
realProblems.answer: The insulators stop the current leaking from the wires into the metal pylon, which would waste energy and make the pylon dangerous to touch.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The insulators stop the current leaking from the wires into the metal pylon, which would waste energy and make the pylon dangerous to touch.

realProblems.prompt: Explain why you should never touch a light switch with wet hands, using the words conductor and current.
realProblems.answer: Wet hands and your body are conductors, so touching a switch with wet hands could let a dangerous current flow through you. Dry skin is a poorer conductor.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Wet hands and your body are conductors, so touching a switch with wet hands could let a dangerous current flow through you. Dry skin is a poorer conductor.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the particles that flow around a circuit when current flows.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Electrons. These are the tiny particles that flow through the wires.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What charge do these particles carry?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A negative charge. This is why they are repelled by the negative terminal.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the job of a cell in a circuit?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The cell provides energy by changing stored chemical energy into electrical energy, which pushes the electrons.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the rule about attracting and repelling charges.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Opposite charges attract (pull together); like charges repel (push apart).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does a break anywhere in a circuit stop the current everywhere?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Electrons can only flow in a complete loop. A break anywhere means the loop is no longer complete, so the flow stops everywhere at once.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What unit is current measured in, and what is its symbol?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Current is measured in amps, symbol A.

questions.question: Electric current is the flow of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: electrons Current is the flow of electrons around the circuit.

questions.question: Which of these must be present for current to flow in a circuit?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a complete loop Electrons can only flow in an unbroken, complete circuit.

questions.question: The negative terminal of a cell:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: pushes electrons out Electrons are negative, so the negative terminal repels (pushes) them out.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: attract Opposite charges attract; like charges repel.

questions.question: Current is measured in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: amps Current is measured in amps, symbol A.

questions.question: An ammeter must be connected:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: in series An ammeter goes in series so all the current flows through it to be counted.

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the same everywhere The golden rule of series circuits: current is equal at every point.

questions.question: Which is a conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: copper Copper is a metal, so its electrons are free to move.

questions.question: Which is an insulator?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: plastic Plastic is a non-metal; its electrons are not free to move.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can explain that electric current is the flow of tiny particles called electrons around a complete circuit.
I can describe the job of a cell and a battery, and identify the positive (+) and negative (-) terminals.
I can explain that opposite charges attract and like charges repel, and use this to say which way electrons move.
I can recognise and draw the standard circuit symbols for a cell, battery, switch, lamp, buzzer and ammeter.
I can draw and read a circuit diagram, and build a real circuit from a diagram.
I can state that current is measured in amps (A) using an ammeter connected in series.
I can state the rule that current is the same at every point in a series circuit.
